steps.title: Identify the independent clauses on each side
steps.content:
The clause before the blank—”Many Farms Chapter, which covers 168,000 acres, is one of the 110 chapters of the Navajo Nation”—is an independent clause.
The clause after the blank—”In Diné bizaad (the Navajo language), the chapter is called Dá'ák'eh Haláni”—is also an independent clause.

steps.title: Apply the rule for separating independent clauses
steps.content:
When two independent clauses appear consecutively, they must be separated by a period, semicolon, or comma with a coordinating conjunction.
Choice B uses a period, properly separating the two clauses into two complete sentences.
Choices A, C, and D create incomplete first clauses by ending with dangling or unattached words.
That matches choice B — B is correct.
